coredata.dc:title: Hemoglobin detection using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Barati, Ali
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Abdollahi, Hamid
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, we have described the application of high fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) without any surface modification as a simple and fast responding fluorescence probe for sensitive and selective determination of hemoglobin (Hb) in the presence of H2O2. Although Hb itself was able to quench the fluorescence of CDs, based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of the protein that affects both excitation and emission spectra of CDs, the presence of H2O2 resulted in further improvement of the sensitivity of Hb detection. The assay is based on the reaction of Hb with H2O2 that generates reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl (
OH
•
) and superoxide (
O
2
• −
) radicals under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb and the subsequent reaction of hydroxyl radicals, as strong oxidizing agents, with CDs resulting in high fluorescence quenching. The proposed probe was used for determination of Hb in concentration range of 1–100nM with a detection limit of 0.4nM. The method was successfully applied to the determination of Hb in human blood samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hemoglobin
Free radicals
Fluorescence probe
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Hb in blood sample
Characterization and optical properties of synthesized CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Normalized UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of CDs (solid lines) and UV–vis absorption of Hb (dash line). Insets show the photographs of prepared CDs in aqueous solution under visible light (left) and 365nm UV lamp light (right). (b) TEM image of CDs.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of CDs upon addition of different Hb concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 50, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500, 2000, 3000, 4000, 6000 and 10000nM. λex=340nm). (b) The dependence of fluorescence intensity at peak values on Hb concentrations. Inset shows linear region from 0 to 6000nM.

Normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs in 0.01M CH3COOH/CH3COONa (pH=5.0) buffer solution and in the presence of H2O2 (1mM), Hb (100nM) and a combination of H2O2 and Hb (from top to bottom). Inset shows CDs solution (right) and CDs solution after addition of H2O2 and Hb (left) under 365nm UV lamp.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of CDS/H2O2 upon addition of different Hb concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 1, 3, 6, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150 and 200nM. λex=340nm). (b) The dependence of fluorescence intensity on Hb concentrations at wavelengths 440nm. Inset shows linear region from 0 to 100nM.

The different fluorescence intensity ratios of the CDS/H2O2 probe in the presence of various potential interferences. Hb (100nM), glucose (10μM), uric acid (10μM), ascorbic acid (10μM), dopamine (10μM), HSA (1μM), cytochrome c (100ng/L), myoglobin (100ng/L), Na+ (0.1mM), K+ (0.1mM), Ca2+ (0.1mM), Mg2+ (0.1mM), Cl− (0.1mM) and HCO3 − (0.1mM). The black and white bars mean the addition of potential interferences and their coexist with Hb (100nM), respectively.
Comparison of different recently published optical methods for the determination of Hb.

row.entry: Interaction of AuCl4− with aniline-2-sulfonic acid
G-quadruplex structure aptamer and reduced graphene oxide mixture

head.ce:title: Hemoglobin detection using carbon dots as a fluorescence probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences, Zanjan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, Institute for Advanced Studies in Basic Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, we have described the application of high fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) without any surface modification as a simple and fast responding fluorescence probe for sensitive and selective determination of hemoglobin (Hb) in the presence of H2O2. Although Hb itself was able to quench the fluorescence of CDs, based on the inner filter effect (IFE) of the protein that affects both excitation and emission spectra of CDs, the presence of H2O2 resulted in further improvement of the sensitivity of Hb detection. The assay is based on the reaction of Hb with H2O2 that generates reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl (
OH
•
) and superoxide (
O
2
• −
) radicals under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb and the subsequent reaction of hydroxyl radicals, as strong oxidizing agents, with CDs resulting in high fluorescence quenching. The proposed probe was used for determination of Hb in concentration range of 1–100nM with a detection limit of 0.4nM. The method was successfully applied to the determination of Hb in human blood samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Hemoglobin (Hb) is a tetrameric metalloprotein consisting a protein part (globin) and four iron-containing parts (heme), which exists in red blood cells. Hb is responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to various parts of the body through blood and carrying the main portion of carbon dioxide from these parts to the lungs. The amount of Hb in blood is associated with many clinical diseases such as leukemia, anemia, heart diseases, etc., while its normal level displays the well-functioning of the organism. The normal level of Hb for males is 13.0–18.0g/dL and for females is 12.0–16.0g/dL (Path, 1997) and Hb amount below these levels causes anemia. It is worth mentioning that about 2 billion people, mainly women and children, worldwide suffer from anemia (Ranganathan and Gunasekaran, 2006).

Therefore, accurate determination of Hb in clinical diagnostics is essential to assess the extent of anemia and to evaluate various intervention programs aimed at control of anemia. The World Health Organization (WHO) has documented numerous available methods for the estimation of Hb level in blood in detail (Path, 1997), each of which possess its own advantages and limitations. Among these methods, the cyanmethemoglobin method is considered as the most accurate and widely used method of Hb measuring. However, these methods are not adequate for low-level detection of Hb. The determination of low concentrations of Hb is of great importance for clinical diagnosis, for example, for detecting hemolysis or describing discrete changes in Hb levels in blood if any due to some fatalities. Thus, there is a strong need for a system of Hb measurement in human blood, which is simple, reasonably accurate, and more sensitive than traditional methods.

In recent years, a number of new analytical methods including fluorimetric (Chen et al., 2013; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2015; Li et al., 2014a; Morgner et al., 2015; Pang et al., 2014; Zo et al., 2014) electrochemical (Matysiak et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2014b; Zhang et al., 2015), colorimetric (Li et al., 2014b; Pourreza and Golmohammadi, 2015) and chemiluminescence (Traore et al., 2013) have been reported for sensitive determination of Hb. However, some of these methods require expensive materials such as DNA aptamers, complicated probe preparation, or use of fluorescent dyes that suffer from photobleaching for sensitive detection of Hb.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) (Baker and Baker, 2010; Li et al., 2012) as a recently class of quantum dots have attracted considerable attention due to their unique optical properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and excellent optical and chemical stability. Similar to semiconductor quantum dots, high attention is paid to the analytical application of the CDs. However, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared CDs is typically more stable toward the chemical/physical environment changes such as pH, ionic strength, or even the presence of many chemical species. So far, only a few direct detection of analytes using CDs has been reported (Liu et al., 2012; Yazid et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Zhou et al., 2012). Thus, indirect detection method could be a promising way to employ the unique advantage of these nanomaterials as fluorescence probes for sensing chemical species that have no direct effect on their fluorescence.

The aim of this work was to develop a low cost, simple, and sensitive method for quantitative determination of low levels of Hb by using CDs as a fluorescence probe. Although, Hb itself can quench the fluorescence of CDs based on inner filter effect (IFE), but the presence of H2O2 leads to further improvement of the sensitivity of Hb detection. Additionally, the quenching mechanisms of CDs with Hb in the presence or absence of H2O2 were found to be completely different and discussed thoroughly.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, Hb powder, myoglobin, cytochrome c, glucose, dopamine, uric acid, ascorbic acid, and human serum albumin (HSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30% (v/v)), NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O and FeCl2·4H2O were purchased from Merck. The working solution of H2O2 was prepared every day with dilution of H2O2 30% with double-distilled water. CH3COONa/CH3COOH buffer solution (0.01M, pH 5.0) was prepared by diluting desire volume of CH3COOH and desire weight of CH3COONa in double-distilled water. All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp. Samples were excited at 340nm, and emission spectra were measured at a wavelength range of 350–650nm. All measurements were performed in a 10mm optical path quartz cell. UV–vis spectra were collected by an Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of N-CQDs were recorded on a Bruker infrared spectrophotometer. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was obtained by a Zeiss EM10C microscope.

The CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method according to a previous published method (Zhu et al., 2013). Briefly, 2g citric acid and 1mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20mL double-distilled water. The solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180°C for 4h. After the heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and the resultant CDs solutions were filtered through a 0.2μm microporous membrane, precipitated using acetone, and dried in oven at 80°C.

The typical detection measurements were carried out as follows: a series of CDs (100µL) and H2O2 (100µL) solutions from their stock solutions were prepared and diluted to 3mL with buffered solution (CH3COONa/CH3COOH, 0.01M, pH 5.0) to achieve the final concentration of 2×10−5 mgmL−1 CDs and 1mM H2O2. Then, 100µL of Hb solution with different concentrations was added into the above mixed solutions. The fluorescence intensity of the solutions was determined after 5min of sonication and another 5min of standing. The same procedure was applied to evaluate the influence of potential interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hb in blood sample
ce:section.ce:para: 10µL of fingertip blood samples was collected from lab volunteers after cleaning their finger skin with alcohol and puncturing the skin deeply enough using a sharp needle. The collected blood samples were diluted to 1mL of double-distilled water and incubated for 20min to completely release the Hb from red blood cells. The sample solutions were then centrifuged (at 1000g) for 10min and 10µL of the supernatants was added into 3mL of buffered solution of CDs/H2O2 for fluorescence detection of Hb.

To compare the results with a standard method, the Hb level was also determined colorimetrically using a cyanmethemoglobin method as an established clinical standard method (Bhaskaram et al., 2003). Briefly, 20µL of blood samples was added to 5mL of Drabkin's solution (0.05g KCN, 0.20g potassium ferricyanide, 1.00g sodium bicarbonate in I L distilled water) and their absorbance was measured at 540nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Normalized UV–vis and emission spectra of as-prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 1a. As can be seen, the prepared CDs possess UV–vis absorption spectrum with a peak at 345nm and a shoulder at 235nm. According to previous studies, the peak at 235nm is ascribed to π–π* transition of aromatic C=C bonds, while a shoulder at 345nm is attributed to n–π* transition of C=O bonds (Yu et al., 2012). The CDs present emission spectra ranging from about 400 to 550nm with a nearly excitation-independent emission behavior and a maximal fluorescence wavelength at 442nm with a full width at half-maximum (FWHM) of around 70nm.

The existence of surface functional groups of CDs was studied using FTIR spectra (Fig. S1). Peaks at 3200–3550cm−1 and 2800–2950cm−1 could be attributed to the C–OH and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively. Stretching and bending vibrations of the N–H could appear at 3000–3300cm−1 and 1400cm−1, respectively. Peaks at about 1600 and 1280cm−1 indicated the presence of C=O and C–NH–C, respectively, and the band at around 1080cm−1 presents the existence of C–O (hydroxyl, ester, epoxide, or ether) groups. Generally, the existence of these functional groups represents excellent water solubility of these nanomaterials without further surface modification. The morphology of prepared CDs was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From the TEM image (Fig. 1b) the CDs are nearly spherical with some irregularities, and the average particle diameter of ≈5nm.

To determine whether CDs could be utilize as a fluorescence probe for direct detection of Hb, the changes in the fluorescence intensity of CDs upon gradual addition of Hb in the rage of 0–10000nM were recorded ( Fig. 2a). As seen, the addition of Hb induces a regular decrease in the fluorescence intensity accompanied by a gradual red-shift (about 20nm) in the emission spectra. On the other hand, a closer look at the absorption spectrum of Hb reveals a considerable spectral overlap with both excitation and emission spectra of CDs (Fig. 1a). As expected, the spectral overlap of Hb absorption with the excitation spectrum of CDs results in an equivalent decrease in the intensity of the entire emission spectral range. At the same time, the spectral overlap of Hb with the emission spectrum leads to higher quenching of the short-wavelength side of the emission spectra, which subsequently results in a red-shifted spectrum. These facts along with the excitation wavelength-dependent of the quenching (not shown), suggest that the quenching of the CDs by Hb can be explained by IFE (Shang and Dong, 2009). In fact, the IFE is an important factor in spectrofluorometry, which results from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by absorbers in the detection system. Although IFE is originally a source of errors in fluorimetry, several recent studies have employed its application in developing fluorescent probes (Shang and Dong, 2009; Shao et al., 2005).

The variations in the fluorescence intensity (I/I 0) of CDs at peak values with increasing amounts of Hb are presented in Fig. 2b, where I 0 and I denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Hb, respectively. The quenching process follows a linear logarithmic behavior with the equation of –log (I/I 0)=1.47×10−4 [Hb]−3.13×10−3 and a correlation coefficient of 0.9955 with concentrations ranging from 0 to 6000nM (Fig. 2b inset). The detection limit was estimated to be 34nM based on triple standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity in the absence of Hb. It should be noted that direct detection of Hb was previously reported (Wang et al., 2014a) using electrochemical synthesized CDs from glycine where the quenching process was demonstrated through a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process.

Interestingly, as can be seen from Fig. 3, the fluorescence shows a negligible change with the addition of 100nM of Hb or 1mM of H2O2 individually, but it is quenched significantly by the mixture of them. Moreover, no evident spectral shift in the emission spectra was observed even at the quenched fluorescence. These observations obviously indicate that the quenching process follows a different mechanism in the presence of H2O2, in which the reaction between Hb and H2O2 probably plays an important role in the fluorescence quenching.

To our knowledge, considerable attention has been focused on the reaction of H2O2 with Hb as an iron-rich substance (Nagababu and Rifkind, 2004). Sadrzadeh et al. (1984) reported that Hb could act as a biologic Fenton catalyst. They attributed the hydroxyl radicals to the active species generated in the course of reaction between Hb and H2O2 and suggested that the mechanism of the reaction is very likely a Fenton-type reaction, wherein ferrous heme iron decomposes H2O2 to form metHb (HbFe3+), hydroxyl radical (
OH
•
), and hydroxyl anion.
(1)
HbFe
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
HbFe
3 +
+
OH
•
+
OH
−

Morover, it has been reported that the reaction of Hb with a molar excess of H2O2 will lead to degradation of heme and the release of iron (Puppo and Halliwell, 1988; Gutteridge, 1986). Gutteridge (1986) concluded that the released iron, not the heme iron, promotes the hydroxyl radical formation in Fenton reaction. In addition, the formation of ferryl-Hb (Hb-Fe4+=O) species as strong oxidant intermediates was also reported during the reaction of Hb with an excess of H2O2 (Puppo and Halliwell, 1988). Nagababu and Rifkind (2000) detected an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) radical signal when H2O2 reacted with Hb and attributed some of which to superoxide radicals (
O
2
• −
) formed by the reaction of ferryl-Hb with H2O2.
(2)
HbFe
2 +
+
H
2
O
2
→
HbFe
4 +
= O +
H
2
O 2
→
HbFe
3 +
+
O 2
• −

However, according to the Haber–Weiss reaction (Kehrer, 2000) the hydroxyl radicals can be generated from the interaction between superoxide and H2O2 catalyzed by certain transition metal ions, specifically iron.
(3)
O 2
• −
+
H 2
O 2
→
Fe
2 +
/
Fe
3 +
O 2
+
OH
•
+
OH
−

In addition, CDs have been recently reported as fluorescence probes to detect the reagents involved in the Fenton or Fenton-like reactions. As example, Wei et al. (2014) used CDs prepared from gelatin to detect H2O2 and glucose in the presence of Fe2+ , and attributed the fluorescence quenching to the hydroxyl radicals produced during the reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+. Ren et al. (2014) used CDs prepared from polyvinylpyrrolidone in detection of hydroxyl radicals generated in the reaction of Cu+ and H2O2. On this subject, some other works are reported but with a different mechanism (Cao et al., 2015; Qian et al., 2014). These reports suggested that H2O2 oxidizes Fe2+ to Fe3+, and subsequent electron transfer between the excited-state of CDs and surface-complexed Fe3+ ions results in the strong fluorescence quenching.

Because Hb is a well-known iron metalloprotein, we first investigated the fluorescent response of CDs with varying concentrations of Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions (Fig. S2). As seen in Fig. S2a, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually with increasing amounts of Fe3+ and quenched about 85% at 1mM level of the of Fe3+. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity quenched only about 20% after the addition of 1mM Fe2+ (Fig. S2b). We also compared the fluorescence quenching efficiencies of Fe2+ and Fe3+ at concentrations of 5µM and H2O2 at 1mM, separately, and a solution containing 1mM of H2O2 and 5µM Fe2+ (Fig. S3). As seen, less than 5% of the probe fluorescence was quenched by Fe2+, Fe3+, or H2O2 individually, while it was almost completely quenched by the combination of H2O2 and Fe2+. Thus, based on these results, it can be concluded that the generated hydroxyl radicals from H2O2 in the presence of Fe2+ are responsible for the fluorescence quenching. The generation of hydroxyl radicals was also confirmed with the pronounce quenching effect of Cu2+/ascorbic acid system in the presence of H2O2 on the fluorescence probe used in this study (Fig. S4). As reported in the literature, in this case, Cu2+ is reduced to Cu+ in the presence of ascorbic acid, which in turn reacts with H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (Ren et al., 2014).

It has been well documented that the reactive oxygen radicals are known for their strong oxidizing nature. Particularly, hydroxyl radicals with the redox potential of 2.74V (vs standard hydrogen electrode, SHE) (Klaning et al., 1985) have extremely strong ability to capture electron (Wu et al., 2009). Meanwhile, it is also reported that the fluorescence properties of CDs are highly affected by their surface states (Dong et al., 2012). Thus, in this work, we can assume that the oxidation of the surface hydroxyl groups of CDs by hydroxyl radicals generated in the solution is able to change the surface states and lead to the fluorescence quenching. To further verify this assumption, the fluorescence change of the CDs in the presence of KMnO4, as a common oxidant, was also investigated (Fig. S5a). The experimental results clearly revealed the high fluorescence quenching of CDs with KMnO4. For more clarification, in the next step, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was added to CDs solutions oxidized with KMnO4 and H2O2/Fe2+ and it was found that, in both cases, the quenched fluorescence was partially recovered (Fig. S5b and c).

Agreeing with the reported results (Nagababu and Rifkind, 2004; Dong et al., 2012) and our experimental observations, a proposed mechanism for fluorescence quenching of CDs/H2O2 probe by Hb was illustrated in Scheme S1. According to this mechanism, the reaction of Hb with H2O2 generates reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl and superoxide radicals under heme degradation and/or iron release from Hb and the subsequent reaction of hydroxyl radicals, as strong oxidizing agents, with CDs leading to the fluorescence quenching. It is worth mentioning that addition of thiourea, as a radical scavenger, to the reaction mixture containing CDs/H2O2 and Hb can prevent the quenching process (Fig. S6), which further verify that the quenching effect is caused by the hydroxyl radical.

Thus, CDs/H2O2 probe can be applied as a new fluorescence probe for quantitative determination of Hb. The time studies of the fluorescence quenching of CDs/H2O2 with the addition of Hb were performed and the results are shown in Fig. S7a. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity continued even after 40min, while it reached a steady state after 5min sonication. Thus, in order to have a fast responding probe, the fluorescence intensity of all reaction mixtures was recorded after 5min sonication followed by 5min standing.

The effect of pH on the probe response was investigated over the range of pH 3.0–5.5 (CH3COOH/CH3COONa buffer solution, 0.01M) and the largest fluorescence quenching efficiency appeared at pH 5.0 (Fig. S7b). The optimum concentration of H2O2 was also found to be 1mM.

Under the optimum conditions, the quantitative determination of Hb using CDS/H2O2 was performed. The fluorescence spectra of the probe in the presence of different concentrations of Hb are shown in Fig. 4a. The dependency of I 0/I on Hb concentration shows a dynamic range of 1–100nM with a linear logarithmic equation with a regression equation −log (I/I 0)=1.0×10−2 [Hb]−7.3×10−3 and a correlation coefficient of 0.9968 (Fig. 4b, inset). The detection limit was estimated to be 0.4nM (3σ, n=5). The detection system shows high reproducibility with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.5% (n=4) at a concentration of 40nM. The linear range and LOD of various recently published optical methods are compared with those of our work ( Table 1). From the table, the Hb assay based on CDs/H2O2 not only has the highest sensitivity, but also needs no expensive materials and complicated probe preparation and provides a simple and easy assay.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed method toward Hb, the interfering experiments were performed. The effects of some commonly occurring interfering species at concentrations higher than their normal values are shown in Fig. 5. These results indicate that these compounds do not pose any serious interference in determination of Hb. The interfering effect of other heme proteins, such as cytochrome c and myoglobin was also investigated (Fig. S8). Since myoglobin and cytochrome c contain only one heme group in their structure it is expected that their effect on the fluorescence must be weaker compared to the same concentration of Hb with four heme groups in its structure. Thus, at 100nM of these proteins, it was found that the fluorescence is quenched about 35% in the cases of myoglobin or cytochrome c, while it is quenched about 90% for Hb. However, their effects on the detection of Hb in blood could be neglected because their concentrations (1.6µg/L and 27µg/L, respectively) (Osaka et al., 2009; Pelsers et al., 1999) are much lower than that of Hb (130–180g/L, in adult males).

The proposed CDS/H2O2 fluorescent probe was applied to the determination of Hb in the blood samples of healthy male and female adult volunteers from our laboratory. The results obtained by the two methods after considering the dilution factor are presented in Table S1. According to the results, the average of three replicates of Hb measurement by the proposed method was in good agreement with those obtained by the standard method.

In this study, CDs in the presence of H2O2 was successfully applied in the fabrication of a fluorescence probe for sensitive determination of Hb in human blood samples. The probe was constructed based on the quenching effect of the generated reactive radicals during the reaction of Hb with H2O2 on the fluorescence of CDs. The proposed probe needs no expensive materials and provides a simple and high reproducible assay. Fluorescence quenching of CDs by free radicals such as hydrogen radicals along with the involvement of these radicals in a number of biological reactions, reveals the potential application of CDs as an excellent fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of various biological compounds.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.04.073.
